texto: It might be well enough to wander if you've a place and people to come back to, but I tell you now there's no desolation like wanting to go home and truly not knowing where it is.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Elizabeth Kerner: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de esforço, persistência e resultados. O trecho “It might be well enough to wander if you've a place and people to come back to, but I tell you no…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que sucesso, home, jamie pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de jamie e wandering.

frase_original: It might be well enough to wander if you've a place and people to come back to, but I tell you now there's no desolation like wanting to go home and truly not knowing where it is.

texto: The Song of the Winged Ones is a song of celebration, written as though the singer were standing on the Dragon Isle watching the dragons flying in the sun. The words are full of wonder at the beauty of the creatures; and there is a curious pause in the middle of one of the stanzas near the end, where the singer waits a full four measures in silence for those who listen to hear the music of distant dragon wings. It seldom fails to bring echoes of something beyond the silence, and is almost never performed because many bards fear it.I love it.
explicacao: Citação de Elizabeth Kerner sobre limites e escolhas conscientes. “The Song of the Winged Ones is a song of celebration, written as though the singer were standing …” articula amor, bard, elizabeth-kerner em registro de amor, com relevância para contextos como reflexao-noturna e termino.
